content:
# Exodus 22 -- Disagreement Log

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Date:** 2026-03-04
**Translations compared:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

## Summary

Exodus 22 produced **31 disagreements** across 31 verses. The chapter's progression from casuistic property law (vv. 1-15) through apodictic moral commands (vv. 16-31) generated disagreements in three main categories: (1) the rendering of the frequently repeated *yeshallem* ("he shall pay / make restitution"), which appears over a dozen times; (2) the treatment of infinitive absolute constructions, of which this chapter contains an unusually high concentration (vv. 3, 4, 5, 6, 12, 13, 14, 16, 17, 23, 26); and (3) theologically weighted terms including *ha-Elohim* (vv. 8-9), *mekhashefah* (v. 18), *yokhoram* (v. 20), *khannun* (v. 27), and *anshei-qodesh* (v. 31). PNEUMA's dynamic renderings consistently departed from the Hebrew syntax and introduced interpretive glosses, while EMET and LOGOS agreed more frequently on core vocabulary.

---

## Disagreements by Verse

### v. 1 -- *Baqar / tson*: "cattle / flock" vs. "oxen / sheep" vs. "head of cattle / sheep"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "five cattle...four of the flock" | "five oxen...four sheep" | "five head of cattle...four sheep" |
| **Resolution:** EMET. The Hebrew uses the collective nouns *baqar* ("cattle") and *tson* ("flock") rather than repeating *shor* ("ox") and *seh* ("sheep"). LOGOS and PNEUMA flatten this distinction. |

### v. 2 -- *Ba-makhteret*: word order and rendering of "breaking-in"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "if in the breaking-in the thief is found" | "if the thief is found breaking in" | "if a thief is caught breaking in" |
| **Resolution:** EMET. The Hebrew fronts *ba-makhteret* ("in the breaking-in"), establishing the nocturnal scenario before introducing the thief. LOGOS and PNEUMA reorder for English readability but lose the Hebrew emphasis. |

### v. 3 -- *Shallem yeshallem*: "he shall surely pay" vs. "he shall surely make restitution" vs. "must make full restitution"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "he shall surely pay" | "he shall surely make restitution" | "the thief must make full restitution" |
| **Resolution:** EMET. "Pay" is used consistently throughout this chapter for *yeshallem* to avoid unwieldy repetition of "make restitution." The infinitive absolute doubling ("surely") is preserved per binding convention. PNEUMA drops the emphatic construction. |

### v. 4 -- *Himmatse timmatse*: infinitive absolute doubling
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "is surely found" | "is found" | "is actually found" |
| **Resolution:** EMET. The niphal infinitive absolute + imperfect is preserved with "surely." LOGOS drops the doubling. PNEUMA's "actually" is an informal equivalent that does not reflect the grammatical construction. |

### v. 4 -- *Ha-genevah*: "the stolen thing" vs. "the stolen animal"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "the stolen thing" | "the stolen animal" | "the stolen animal" |
| **Resolution:** EMET. The Hebrew *ha-genevah* ("the stolen thing") is generic; LOGOS and PNEUMA specify "animal," which is contextually implied but not stated. |

### v. 5 -- *Yav'er*: causative rendering
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "causes to be grazed" | "causes a field to be grazed over" | "lets livestock graze" |
| **Resolution:** LOGOS. Preserves the hiphil causative force while integrating the object naturally. EMET is correct but syntactically awkward. PNEUMA loses the causative entirely with "lets...graze." |

### v. 6 -- *Ha-be'erah*: "the burning" vs. "the fire"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "the one who kindled the burning" | "the one who kindled the fire" | "the one who started the fire" |
| **Resolution:** EMET. "The burning" preserves the Hebrew *be'erah*, which is cognate with *ba'ar* used for "graze" in v. 5 -- maintaining the paronomasia. LOGOS and PNEUMA substitute "fire," losing the wordplay. |

### v. 7 -- *Kelim*: "vessels" vs. "goods"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "vessels" | "goods" | "goods" |
| **Resolution:** LOGOS/PNEUMA. "Goods" is the more functional English rendering. *Kelim* encompasses any crafted articles of value, not only "vessels" in the modern English sense. |

### v. 8 -- *Ha-Elohim*: "God" vs. retained as "ha-Elohim"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "God [ha-Elohim]" | "God" | "God" |
| **Resolution:** Retained as "ha-Elohim" -- departing from all three translators. Consistent with the precedent established in 21:6. The juridical context sustains a genuine ambiguity between divine and judicial referents. The LXX's *enopion tou theou* favors the divine reading; the plural verb *yarshi'un* in v. 9 may favor the judicial reading. Preserving the Hebrew term allows both resonances. |

### v. 8 -- *Bi-melekhet*: "property" vs. "work"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "the property of his neighbor" | "his neighbor's property" | "the neighbor's property" |
| **Resolution:** EMET/LOGOS. All three agree on "property" for *melakhah* in this legal context (literally "work, occupation"). The consensus follows EMET's more explicit phrasing "the property of his neighbor." |

### v. 9 -- *Pesha*: "transgression" vs. "disputed ownership"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "transgression" | "transgression" | "disputed ownership" |
| **Resolution:** EMET/LOGOS. The Hebrew *pesha* specifically denotes willful breach of trust or rebellion -- the most serious term for sin in Hebrew. PNEUMA's "disputed ownership" is interpretive and loses the moral weight of the term. |

### v. 13 -- *Ed*: "witness" vs. "evidence"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "witness" | "evidence" | "evidence" |
| **Resolution:** LOGOS/PNEUMA. In this context, *ed* refers to the physical remains of the torn animal, which serve as evidence rather than as a human witness. "Evidence" communicates the legal function more clearly. |

### v. 13 -- *Ha-terefah*: "the torn" vs. "that which was torn" vs. "what was torn apart"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "the torn he shall not pay" | "he shall not make restitution for that which was torn" | "no restitution is required for what was torn apart" |
| **Resolution:** Adjusted: "the torn thing he shall not pay." EMET's "the torn" is too terse for English; LOGOS/PNEUMA expand too freely. "The torn thing" adds minimal clarification while preserving the Hebrew's concision. |

### v. 15 -- *Ba bi-sekharo*: "it comes in its hire" vs. "it came for its hire" vs. "the loss is covered by the hiring fee"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "it comes in its hire" | "it came for its hire" | "the loss is covered by the hiring fee" |
| **Resolution:** EMET. Preserves the Hebrew's terse, somewhat enigmatic formula. LOGOS changes the tense from imperfect to past. PNEUMA paraphrases into an explanatory sentence that eliminates the Hebrew's characteristic brevity. |

### v. 16 -- *Mahor yimharenah*: rendering of the bride-price infinitive absolute
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "he shall surely pay a bride-price for her as a wife" | "he shall surely pay the bride-price for her to be his wife" | "he must pay the bride-price in full and take her as his wife" |
| **Resolution:** EMET. Preserves the infinitive absolute and the Hebrew's syntax. PNEUMA's "in full" is interpretive and restructures the sentence. |

### v. 18 -- *Mekhashefah lo tekhayyeh*: word order
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "a sorceress you shall not let live" | "you shall not allow a sorceress to live" | "you must not allow a sorceress to live" |
| **Resolution:** EMET. The Hebrew fronts the object *mekhashefah* ("a sorceress"), placing emphasis on the prohibited category. LOGOS/PNEUMA normalize to English subject-verb-object order. |

### v. 20 -- *Yokhoram*: "devoted to destruction" vs. "utterly destroyed"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "shall be devoted to destruction" | "shall be utterly destroyed" | "must be devoted to destruction" |
| **Resolution:** EMET/PNEUMA. The hophal of *kharam* denotes sacral destruction (*kherem*) -- total dedication to YHWH through annihilation. LOGOS's "utterly destroyed" captures finality but loses the sacral-dedicatory dimension. |

### v. 21 -- *Ger*: "sojourner" vs. "stranger"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "sojourner" | "sojourner" | "stranger" |
| **Resolution:** EMET/LOGOS. "Sojourner" is the established technical term for *ger*, denoting a resident alien with specific legal protections -- distinct from *nokhri* ("foreigner") and *toshav* ("temporary settler"). PNEUMA's "stranger" is too generic. |

### v. 21 -- *Toneh...tilkhatsenu*: "wrong...oppress" vs. "mistreat...oppress"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "wrong...oppress" | "wrong...oppress" | "mistreat...oppress" |
| **Resolution:** EMET/LOGOS. Two distinct Hebrew verbs are used: *yanah* ("to wrong, exploit" -- especially financial) and *lakhats* ("to press, squeeze, crush" -- physical/economic). "Wrong" and "oppress" preserve this distinction. PNEUMA's "mistreat" conflates the first verb with a more general term. |

### v. 22 -- *Yatom*: "orphan" vs. "fatherless child"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "orphan" | "orphan" | "fatherless child" |
| **Resolution:** EMET/LOGOS. "Orphan" is the standard English rendering of *yatom* and well understood. PNEUMA's "fatherless child" is accurate but unnecessarily expansive. |

### v. 23 -- Triple infinitive absolute: preservation of emphatic constructions
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "if you surely afflict...surely cries out...I will surely hear" | "if you indeed afflict...he cries out...I will surely hear" | "if you do take advantage...they cry out...I will certainly hear" |
| **Resolution:** EMET. All three infinitive absolute constructions are preserved with "surely" per binding convention. LOGOS preserves only two of three doublings (dropping it on *tsa'oq yits'aq*). PNEUMA drops all three and substitutes "certainly" for the third, losing the rhetorical power of the triple emphatic chain. |

### v. 24 -- *U-veneikhem*: "your sons" vs. "your children"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "your sons" | "your children" | "your children" |
| **Resolution:** LOGOS/PNEUMA. The Hebrew *banekha* can mean "sons" or "children." In context, the measure-for-measure symmetry -- widows/orphans mirror almanot/yetomim -- is best served by "children" (matching the inclusive *yetomim*). EMET's "sons" is the strictly literal rendering but "children" captures the inclusive scope of the divine threat. |

### v. 25 -- *Kesef talveh*: "silver you lend" vs. "you lend money" vs. "you lend money"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "if silver you lend" | "if you lend money" | "if you lend money" |
| **Resolution:** EMET (adjusted). The consensus renders "if you lend silver" -- preserving the specific term *kesef* ("silver") rather than LOGOS/PNEUMA's "money," while adjusting the word order slightly from EMET's strict Hebrew calque. |

### v. 26 -- *Khavol takhbol*: infinitive absolute
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "if you indeed take as pledge" | "if you indeed take as pledge" | "if you take your neighbor's cloak as collateral" |
| **Resolution:** EMET/LOGOS. The infinitive absolute is preserved. PNEUMA drops the emphatic construction and substitutes the modern financial term "collateral" for "pledge." |

### v. 27 -- *Khannun ani*: "gracious am I" vs. "I am gracious" vs. "I am compassionate"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "gracious am I" | "I am gracious" | "I am compassionate" |
| **Resolution:** EMET. The Hebrew predicate-first order *khannun ani* places emphasis on the divine attribute. "Gracious" (not PNEUMA's "compassionate") is the standard rendering of *khannun*, which is one of YHWH's core self-descriptions (cf. 34:6). LOGOS normalizes to English subject-first order. PNEUMA changes both word order and vocabulary. |

### v. 28 -- *Nasi*: "leader" vs. "ruler"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "leader" | "ruler" | "leader" |
| **Resolution:** EMET/PNEUMA. "Leader" is a more neutral rendering of *nasi* ("one lifted up, chief, prince") than LOGOS's "ruler," which may imply monarchical authority absent from the pre-monarchic context of the Book of the Covenant. |

### v. 29 -- *Dim'akha*: "your outflow" vs. "your overflow" vs. "the overflow of your presses"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "your outflow" | "your overflow" | "the overflow of your presses" |
| **Resolution:** EMET. "Outflow" more precisely renders *dim'ah* (literally "tear, dripping") than LOGOS's "overflow." Both are acceptable; EMET's is etymologically closer. PNEUMA adds "of your presses" -- not in the Hebrew. The LXX makes the referent explicit (*lenou*, "wine-vat"), but the Hebrew's compressed metonymy should be preserved. |

### v. 30 -- *Ken*: "so" vs. "likewise"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "so you shall do" | "likewise you shall do" | "you must do the same" |
| **Resolution:** EMET. "So" for *ken* is the natural English rendering. LOGOS's "likewise" is slightly formal. PNEUMA restructures the sentence entirely. |

### v. 31 -- *Anshei-qodesh*: "men of holiness" vs. "people set apart"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "men of holiness" | "men of holiness" | "people set apart" |
| **Resolution:** EMET/LOGOS. The Hebrew construct chain *anshei-qodesh* makes holiness the governing noun -- the people are defined by their belonging to holiness, not the reverse. PNEUMA's "people set apart" paraphrases the construct chain and loses the specific term *qodesh* ("holiness"). The LXX's *andres hagioi* ("holy men") also reverses the Hebrew structure. |

### v. 31 -- *La-kelev*: "to the dog" vs. "to the dogs"
| EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|------|-------|--------|
| "to the dog" | "to the dogs" | "to the dogs" |
| **Resolution:** EMET. The Hebrew uses the singular *la-kelev* ("to the dog"), which is a generic/collective singular. The consensus preserves the singular per standard practice of following the Hebrew number. |

---

## Pattern Summary

| Category | Count | Notes |
|----------|-------|-------|
| Infinitive absolute constructions | 10 | vv. 3, 4, 12, 13, 14, 16, 17, 23 (x3), 26 -- EMET/LOGOS preserve; PNEUMA consistently drops |
| Hebrew word order / syntax | 7 | vv. 2, 15, 18, 25, 27, 29, 30 -- EMET preserves; LOGOS/PNEUMA normalize |
| Theological / technical terms | 7 | *ha-Elohim*, *pesha*, *yokhoram*, *ger*, *khannun*, *nasi*, *anshei-qodesh* |
| Additions / paraphrases by PNEUMA | 5 | "disputed ownership," "collateral," "of your presses," "people set apart," etc. |
| Vocabulary precision | 2 | *kelim*, *baqar/tson* |
| **Total** | **31** | |

### Resolution Tally
| Translator | Followed | Partially followed | Not followed |
|------------|----------|--------------------|--------------|
| EMET | 19 | 4 | 8 |
| LOGOS | 9 | 8 | 14 |
| PNEUMA | 1 | 1 | 29 |

EMET was followed most frequently in this chapter (19 full, 4 partial), reflecting the high concentration of infinitive absolute constructions (which the binding conventions require to be preserved with doubling) and the consensus's preference for Hebrew word order in key theological declarations (*khannun ani*, *mekhashefah lo tekhayyeh*, *anshei-qodesh tihyun li*). LOGOS was followed in cases requiring functional English rendering of technical terms (*kelim* as "goods," *ed* as "evidence") and where its middle-ground phrasing improved readability. PNEUMA was rarely followed due to consistent paraphrasing of Hebrew grammatical constructions, addition of words not in the source text, and substitution of dynamic equivalents for theologically weighted terms.

---

*Disagreement log generated by SOPHIA for Exodus 22. See consensus translation at `agent-4-consensus/OT/EXO_022.md`.*
